questions.prompt: Select all that are true about what a single-turn eval measures.
choices: It grades one response to one prompt in isolation, with no state carried across turns.
It tells you whether the model answers well when everything it needs is already present at once.
It verifies that a value produced on an early turn is still reused correctly many turns later.
It measures the backend state left behind after a long agentic session.
questions.explanation: A single-turn eval hands the model one prompt and grades one response in isolation, so it checks isolated answering but never cross-turn reuse or accumulated backend state.

questions.prompt: Which statements about a multi-turn agentic task are true?
choices: The conversation carries state across several turns
A tool call can span turns, with the result arriving on a later turn
The agent may need to reuse an earlier tool result on a later turn
Every turn is graded fully in isolation from the others
questions.explanation: Multi-turn tasks carry state, let tool calls span turns, and require reusing earlier results. Grading each turn in isolation is the single-turn model, not the multi-turn one.

questions.prompt: Which of these are accurate about the research grounding for the single-turn versus multi-turn gap?
choices: The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard V3 added multi-turn categories that check backend state across turns
The FunctionGemma docs state that multi-turn and multi-step workflows are not supported
The FunctionGemma docs state that single-turn and parallel tool calls are supported
questions.explanation: All three are accurate. BFCL V3 added multi-turn state checks, and the FunctionGemma docs support single-turn and parallel calls while explicitly excluding multi-turn workflows.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are TRUE of a single-turn eval, as opposed to a multi-turn agentic task?
choices: It carries state from an earlier turn into a later turn
It grades a tool call that spans multiple turns
It checks that a value produced early is reused correctly later
questions.explanation: None are true of a single-turn eval. Those are all properties of the multi-turn agentic task. A single-turn eval grades one response in isolation.

questions.prompt: Put the turns of the failing multi-turn task in the order they happen.
items: The user asks the agent to complete a task
The agent calls a lookup tool
The tool returns an order id on a later turn
The agent must reuse the order id but drops it and fails
correct_order: The user asks the agent to complete a task
The agent calls a lookup tool
The tool returns an order id on a later turn
The agent must reuse the order id but drops it and fails

questions.prompt: Explain how a model can pass every single-turn function-calling question and still ship as a broken agent. Include both the technical reason and the organizational reason from the lesson.
questions.actual_answer: Technically, a single-turn eval grades one response in isolation, so it only checks whether the model answers well when everything it needs is already present. A multi-turn agentic task is different because state has to survive across turns and a tool call can span turns. The model requests a tool on one turn, gets the result on the next, and must reuse that result on a later turn. That carry-forward step is exactly what the single-turn eval never exercises, so the model can be perfect on every single-turn question and still drop the value across turns and fail the real task. The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard V3 added multi-turn categories that check backend state across turns for this reason, and the FunctionGemma docs confirm single-turn and parallel calls are supported while multi-turn workflows are not. Organizationally, the post-training team and the evals team disagreed over whether multi-turn was even a supported use case, so no one owned the multi-turn eval. An unowned eval left a blind spot, and the green single-turn score shipped a broken agent.
questions.rubric: Full credit: names the technical reason (single-turn grades one response in isolation while multi-turn needs a value to survive across turns and a tool call to span turns, so the carry-forward step is untested) AND the organizational reason (two teams disagreed on scope, so no one owned the multi-turn eval). Bonus for citing BFCL V3 or the FunctionGemma docs. Partial credit: gives the technical reason or the organizational reason but not both. Low credit: vague or only restates that the eval passed.
